section.title: Paul Accepted by the Apostles
section.sectionRationale: Paul continues his defense by describing his visit to Jerusalem. He set his gospel before the recognized leaders, resisted the demand that Titus be circumcised, and stood firm against false brothers who wanted to enslave the Gentile believers. The pillars added nothing to his gospel and extended the right hand of fellowship. The truth of the gospel was preserved for the Galatians in that very meeting.
section.thesis: When Paul laid his gospel before the Jerusalem leaders, they added nothing to it and recognized the grace given to him, proving that there is one gospel for Jew and Gentile alike, and that the demand to circumcise Gentiles was rejected so that the truth of the gospel might remain.
section.frameNote: Three movements: Paul sets his gospel before the leaders and refuses to have Titus compelled to be circumcised; the false brothers who tried to enslave believers and Paul's refusal to yield; and the pillars recognizing Paul's calling and giving the right hand of fellowship.

units.label: The gospel set before the leaders
units.note: Titus not compelled to be circumcised

verses.text: Fourteen years later I went up again to Jerusalem, accompanied by Barnabas. I took Titus along also.

I went in response to a revelation and set before them the gospel that I preach among the Gentiles. But I spoke privately to those recognized as leaders, for fear that I was running or had already run in vain.
Yet not even Titus, who was with me, was compelled to be circumcised, even though he was a Greek.

units.label: False brothers and the truth of the gospel
units.note: we did not give in for a moment

verses.text: This issue arose because some false brothers had come in under false pretenses to spy on our {{eleutheria|freedom}} in Christ Jesus, in order to enslave us.
We did not give in to them for a moment, so that the truth of the gospel would remain with you.

units.label: The right hand of fellowship
units.note: the pillars added nothing, only remember the poor

verses.text: But as for the highly esteemed, whatever they were makes no difference to me; God does not show favoritism, those leaders added nothing to me.
On the contrary, they saw that I had been entrusted to preach the gospel to the uncircumcised, just as Peter had been to the circumcised.
For the One who was at work in Peter's apostleship to the circumcised was also at work in my apostleship to the Gentiles.

And recognizing the {{charis|grace}} that I had been given, James, Cephas, and John, those reputed to be pillars, gave me and Barnabas the right hand of {{koinonia|fellowship}}, so that we should go to the Gentiles, and they to the circumcised.
They only asked us to remember the poor, the very thing I was eager to do.

eleutheria.gloss: Freedom, liberty. Here it is the freedom believers have in Christ Jesus, released from the yoke of law keeping as the ground of acceptance with God. The false brothers came precisely to spy out this freedom in order to enslave believers again, which is why Paul would not yield for a moment. Freedom in Christ is a recurring banner of the letter.

koinonia.gloss: Fellowship, partnership, a sharing in common. The right hand of koinonia was a public gesture of recognition and shared mission: the Jerusalem pillars affirmed that Paul and Barnabas preached the same gospel and were partners in the same work, though sent to different peoples. It signals unity of gospel across the divide of Jew and Gentile.

charis.gloss: Grace, God's unearned favor, here also the enabling favor given to Paul for his ministry. The pillars recognized the charis that had been given to Paul, verse 9. His commission to the Gentiles was itself a gift of grace, not a human appointment, which is why they welcomed rather than corrected him.

basic.context: Paul keeps telling his story to prove his gospel is God's. Years after his conversion he went to Jerusalem and laid out the gospel he preached to Gentiles. A test case came up: should Titus, a Greek believer, be circumcised? Paul refused to give in, because the freedom of the gospel was at stake. The leaders recognized God's grace on Paul, added nothing to his message, and shook hands in partnership.
basic.hermeneutics: This is still autobiography serving an argument. The point is twofold: the gospel is one, affirmed by the Jerusalem leaders, and it does not require Gentiles to be circumcised. Read the Titus episode as a deliberate line in the sand: to add circumcision would be to lose the gospel.
basic.originalLanguages: At the center is eleutheria, freedom in Christ, which the false brothers tried to spy out and destroy. The leaders recognized the charis, the grace, given to Paul and offered koinonia, fellowship, a partnership in the one gospel across the Jew and Gentile divide.
basic.typology: The inclusion of the uncircumcised Titus without circumcision anticipates the great truth that in Christ the dividing wall between Jew and Gentile comes down. The one people of God, promised to Abraham as a blessing to all nations, begins to take visible shape as Gentiles are received on the basis of grace alone.
basic.christ: Everything turns on freedom in Christ Jesus. Because Christ has accomplished salvation, acceptance with God rests on Him and not on the badge of circumcision. The same Christ who worked through Peter among Jews worked through Paul among Gentiles, so the one Lord grounds the one gospel.
basic.god: God shows no favoritism, verse 6. He does not measure people by status or reputation, and He entrusts the gospel to whom He wills. It is God who was at work in both Peter and Paul, and it is the grace of God that the pillars recognized. The unity of the mission rests on the one God who saves by grace.
basic.crossReferences: Acts 15 records the Jerusalem council settling the circumcision question. Ephesians 2 says Christ has broken down the dividing wall between Jew and Gentile. Acts 10 shows God accepting Gentiles apart from the law.
basic.oneStory: God promised Abraham that all nations would be blessed through him. Here that promise advances: Gentiles are welcomed on the basis of grace, without becoming Jews first. The one gospel unites a divided humanity, a preview of the new people God is gathering in Christ.

context.literary: The second half of Paul's autobiographical defense. It moves from the private setting of the gospel before the leaders, to the sharp conflict over Titus and the false brothers, to the public recognition and handshake. The repeated language of the truth of the gospel and adding nothing frames the whole.
context.historical: Jerusalem was the mother church, and its leaders James, Peter, and John carried great weight. The pressure to circumcise Gentile converts was intense, and the false brothers likely wanted to bind Gentile Christians to the full law of Moses. Paul's stand over Titus was a concrete test of whether the gospel of grace would hold.
context.canonical: This account stands alongside Acts 15 as evidence that the earliest church wrestled with and settled the question of Gentile inclusion. It grounds the New Testament conviction that Jew and Gentile are saved on the same terms, by grace through faith in Christ.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that the Jerusalem leaders recognized Paul's gospel and calling, added nothing to his message, and refused to require circumcision of Gentiles, so that the truth of the gospel of grace was preserved for churches like Galatia.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes a historic meeting and its outcome. The prescriptive weight is in the principle it establishes: the gospel must not be compromised by adding requirements, and believers must not give in even for a moment when the truth of the gospel is at stake.

debates.question: How does this visit relate to the council in Acts 15?

views.label: Same event, different vantage point
views.case: Many take Galatians 2 and Acts 15 as the same Jerusalem meeting, described by Paul from his personal angle and by Luke from the church's.

views.label: An earlier famine relief visit
views.case: Others match Galatians 2 with the visit of Acts 11, placing it before the formal council, which better fits Paul's timeline of fourteen years.

debates.bearing: Either way the theological point is unchanged: the Jerusalem leaders affirmed Paul's gospel and did not require Gentile circumcision, upholding salvation by grace.

debates.question: Why did Paul circumcise Timothy later but refuse it for Titus here?

views.label: A matter of principle versus freedom
views.case: Titus's case was a demand imposed as necessary for salvation, which Paul resisted; Timothy's later circumcision was a free choice for the sake of mission, not a requirement of the gospel.

views.label: Different circumstances entirely
views.case: The situations differed in setting and pressure, so the two decisions are consistent rather than contradictory.

debates.bearing: The distinction guards the gospel: circumcision is nothing in itself, but it must never be imposed as a condition of being accepted by God.

study.originalLanguages: The battle is over eleutheria, freedom in Christ, which the false brothers infiltrated the church to spy out and crush by re-imposing the law. Paul would not surrender it for a single moment, so that the truth of the gospel would remain with the Galatians. The happy result was that the leaders recognized the charis, the grace, given to Paul and extended koinonia, fellowship, the right hand of partnership. Two words, freedom and fellowship, capture the passage: the freedom of the gospel defended, and the fellowship of those who preach it affirmed.

typology.fulfillment: Anticipates the breaking down of the wall between Jew and Gentile in Christ, the one new people foreshadowed in the promise to Abraham.

typology.type: One gospel entrusted to Peter and Paul
typology.fulfillment: Points to the one Shepherd gathering one flock from Jew and Gentile alike, fulfilling the promise that all nations would be blessed.

study.christ: The freedom Paul defends is freedom in Christ Jesus. Because Christ has done everything needed for salvation, the badge of circumcision cannot be added as a requirement without denying His sufficiency. The same Christ who worked through Peter's ministry to the circumcised worked through Paul's to the Gentiles, so the unity of the church rests on the one Lord who saves both peoples on identical terms, by grace through faith in Him.
study.god: God does not show favoritism, verse 6, judging no one by outward status or reputation. He is the God who entrusts the gospel to whom He chooses and who was at work in both Peter and Paul. The grace the pillars recognized in Paul was God's grace. The whole scene magnifies a God who saves Jew and Gentile alike by grace and refuses to let human distinctions decide who belongs to Him.

crossReferences.note: The Jerusalem council deciding that Gentiles need not be circumcised to be saved.
Christ breaking down the dividing wall to make one new people from Jew and Gentile.
God shows no favoritism, pouring out the Spirit on Gentiles apart from the law.
The promise that all nations would be blessed through Abraham, now advancing among the Gentiles.

study.oneStory: God's ancient promise was that through Abraham all the families of the earth would be blessed. In this Jerusalem meeting that promise takes a decisive step forward: Gentiles are welcomed into God's people on the basis of grace alone, without first becoming Jews. The one gospel binds a once divided humanity into one fellowship, a foretaste of the countless multitude from every nation gathered around Christ.

sermon.title: At Stake: The Truth of the Gospel (Galatians 2:1 to 10)
sermon.why: Begg shows how the truth of the gospel itself was at stake in the Jerusalem meeting, why Paul refused to compromise over Titus, and how the leaders recognized the one gospel of grace for Jew and Gentile alike.

quiz.q: Why would Paul not allow Titus to be compelled to be circumcised?

options.text: Because the freedom and truth of the gospel were at stake.
options.note: To require circumcision would add to grace and enslave believers.

options.text: Because circumcision was physically dangerous.
options.note: The issue was theological, not medical.

options.text: Because Titus personally disliked the idea.
options.note: The refusal defended the gospel, not merely a preference.

options.text: Because Paul opposed all Jewish customs.
options.note: Paul did not oppose the customs themselves, only imposing them as a condition of salvation.

quiz.q: Who were the false brothers, and what did they want?

options.text: Infiltrators who spied on the believers' freedom in order to enslave them.
options.note: Verse 4 describes their aim to re-impose the law's yoke.

options.text: Sincere teachers with a slightly different emphasis.
options.note: Paul calls them false brothers with false pretenses, not sincere teachers.

options.text: Roman officials investigating the church.
options.note: They came in as pretended brothers, not civil authorities.

options.text: Gentile believers resisting the apostles.
options.note: They pushed law keeping on Gentiles, the opposite of resisting it.

quiz.q: What did the Jerusalem pillars add to Paul's gospel?

options.text: Nothing; they recognized his calling and gave the right hand of fellowship.
options.note: Verses 6 and 9 stress they added nothing and affirmed him.

options.text: A requirement that Gentiles keep the whole law.
options.note: The opposite; they did not impose the law on Gentiles.

options.text: A demand that Paul stop preaching to Gentiles.
options.note: They sent him to the Gentiles with their blessing.

options.text: A rebuke for preaching without permission.
options.note: They welcomed him as a partner, not a rogue.

quiz.q: What does verse 6 say about God's view of status?

options.text: God does not show favoritism.
options.note: Human reputation carries no weight with God.

options.text: God favors those with the highest standing.
options.note: The verse says the opposite; He shows no favoritism.

options.text: God prefers the poor over the influential.
options.note: The point is impartiality, not a reversed favoritism.

options.text: God judges people by their titles.
options.note: Titles make no difference to God.

quiz.q: What one thing did the pillars ask Paul to remember?

options.text: The poor, which Paul was already eager to do.
options.note: Verse 10 records the single request, care for the poor.

options.text: To keep the food laws carefully.
options.note: No such requirement was added; only remembering the poor.

options.text: To send money to the apostles personally.
options.note: The concern was the poor, not the apostles' income.

options.text: To stop associating with Gentiles.
options.note: They affirmed his mission to the Gentiles.

questions: Paul refused to give in even for a moment when the truth of the gospel was at stake. Where might faithfulness require you to hold a clear line rather than compromise?
The pillars recognized the grace God had given Paul rather than his status. How well do you discern God's work in people regardless of their reputation?
Freedom in Christ was worth defending against those who would enslave believers again. What forms of religious slavery are you tempted to return to?
The one request was to remember the poor. How does caring for the poor fit with a robust commitment to the gospel of grace?
God shows no favoritism. How might that truth reshape the way your church welcomes people who are different from you?

translationNotes.note: The BSB brackets a clause with em dashes here; they are rendered with commas, with no change to the wording. God does not show favoritism translates a Hebraic idiom, literally God does not accept the face of man.
The BSB sets off those reputed to be pillars with em dashes; here rendered with commas. Cephas is the Aramaic name for Peter.
The right hand of fellowship renders koinonia, a public gesture of shared partnership in the one gospel, sending Paul to the Gentiles and the pillars to the circumcised.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Galatians 2:1-10. Em dashes in verses 6 and 9 have been rendered as commas to comply with the no-dash style rule; wording is unchanged. Sermon verified this session from the Truth For Life series A Study in Galatians, Volume 1 by Alistair Begg.
